title: Pine tar cold-process soap
subtitle: A traditional remedy bar with 5% pine tar added at trace for dry scalp and skin conditions
excerpt: Pine tar soap has been made in Scandinavia and Northern Europe for centuries. The tar's distinctive smoky, woody scent mellows considerably with a long cure. At 5% it delivers the characteristic properties associated with pine tar without overwhelming the bar.
sourceNotes: Pine tar soap tradition documented in Scandinavian folk medicine and soap-making references. Modern formulation based on Soap Queen and Brambleberry guides for pine tar usage in cold-process soap.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 kg soap batter plus 50 g pine tar (~7 x 130 g bars after 6-week cure)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Pine tar is calculated as an add-in at trace, not in the lye calculation.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Pine tar bars benefit most from a 6 to 8 week cure. The scent mellows and the bar hardens considerably.
recipe.temperatureNote: Combine oils and lye solution at 40 to 45 degrees Celsius. Warm pine tar slightly to make it pourable.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years from cure date. Pine tar extends bar stability. Discard if rancid smell develops or DOS appears.

recipeTools.notes: Pine tar stains; wear gloves during the entire process

glossaryTerms.definition: A thick, dark substance produced by the carbonisation of pine wood. In soap making it is added at trace as an active ingredient. It has a long history of use for dry, itchy, or flaky scalp and skin conditions.
The percentage of oils left unsaponified in a soap formula, providing a conditioning margin.
The emulsification point in cold-process soap making at which the batter holds a visible drizzle on its surface. Add-ins such as pine tar are stirred in at trace.
The 4 to 8 week resting period after unmoulding during which water evaporates, saponification completes, and the soap's scent and colour stabilise.
The chemical reaction between oils and an alkali that produces soap and glycerol.

content.text: Pine tar soap is a proper old-fashioned remedy bar. The ingredient that gives it its name,
, is thick, dark, and pungent straight from the can. At 5% of the total batch weight it is effective without making the bar smell like a campfire. Added at
rather than in the oil phase, it avoids the
process entirely and remains as an active in the finished bar. The

for the best result. Shelf life is 2 years from cure date. Pine tar stains everything it touches; wear old clothes and dedicated gloves.
This recipe uses sodium hydroxide. Read the full
technique. Wear protective goggles and gloves throughout. Pine tar is also an irritant to mucous membranes; work in a ventilated area.

items.prepNote: 5% of total batch weight; added at trace, not in oil phase

content.text: technique. Melt and combine the oils. Prepare the lye solution. Combine at 40 to 45 degrees Celsius and stick-blend to light trace. Gently warm the pine tar if it is too viscous to pour (set the jar in a bowl of warm water for 10 minutes). Stir the pine tar into the batter at trace until evenly distributed. Pour into the mould, cover, and insulate for 48 hours.
Unmould and cut. The bar will be dark brown. Cure on an open rack for 6 to 8 weeks. The smell transforms significantly during this period.
Cured bars keep for 2 years in cool, dry storage. Wrap in brown paper to protect light-coloured surfaces. Discard if the bar smells rancid or develops orange spotting.
